content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 21, 1988

James A. Reuben
Reuben, Quint and Valkevich
595 Market Street, Suite 1330
San Francisco, CA 94105

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-88-369

Dear Mr. Reuben:

You have requested advice regarding your campaign disclosure
requirements under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

May a recipient committee receive as a contribution the
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who made
payments to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser
did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their
payments would be used for political purposes?

CONCLUSION

A recipient committee may receive as a contribution the
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who
contributed to the fundraiser if the contributors to the
fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of
their payments would be used for political purposes.

FACTS

The San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S
Committee (the "Proposition S Committee"), for which you serve as
the treasurer, received a contribution of $68,927.28 from the San
Franciscans for the Homeporting of the USS Missouri (the
"Homeporting Committee"). The Homeporting Committee held a
fundraising event to launch a media campaign to generate awareness
and support for homeporting the USS Missouri in San Francisco
before Proposition S was proposed as and became a ballot measure.

1/Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

James A. Reuben
Page 2


There was no representation to any person who contributed to the
fundraiser sponsored by the Homeporting Committee that any
contribution would be used to support or to oppose a ballot
measure.

ANALYSIS

The issue you raise is the detail of your disclosure
obligation as treasurer for the Proposition S Committee.
Specifically, you want to know if the Proposition S Committee must
identify each person who contributed $100 or more to a fundraiser
which was held by the Homeporting Committee prior to the formation
of your committee and before there was a ballot question.

A contribution is a payment which has been made by the donor
for political purposes. (Section 82015.) A payment is made for
political purposes if it is for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the
qualification or passage of any measure. (Regulation 18215, copy
enclosed.)

Donations received by an organization, like the Homeporting
Committee, which is not formed or existing primarily for political
purposes are "contributions" for purposes of the Act only when, at
the time of making the payment the donor "knows or has reason to
know that the payment or funds with which the payment will be
commingled will be used to make contributions...expenditures."
(Regulation 18215(c).) In your letter, you indicate that the
Homeporting Committee did not make any representations to
potential contributors that they were making payments for the
political purposes of supporting or opposing a ballot measure or
any other ballot question. Based on this information, we conclude
that the persons who contributed to the fundraiser did not intend
to make payments for the purpose of attempting to influence voters
for or against the qualification or passage of Proposition S.

Therefore, the Proposition S Committee may receive the
proceeds of the fundraiser as a contribution from the Homeporting
Committee without being required to disclose the identity of each
person who contributed $100 or more to the fundraiser held by the
Homeporting Committee. The Committee should be listed
as the source of the contribution of $68,927.28.

If the Homeporting Committee is not a recipient committee
pursuant to Section 82013(a) which has obtained an identification
number from the Secretary or State, as we understand is the case,
the Proposition S Committee is required to notify the Homeporting
Committee that it may be required to file campaign reports.
(Section 84105.) The notice is required to be sent within two
weeks of receiving the contribution, so if your Committee has not
already sent a notice to the Homeporting Committee it should do so
promptly.

James A. Reuben
Page 3


If you have further questions, you may contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

By: Bruce W. Robeck
Political Reform Consultant

LAW OFFICES OF

REUBEN, QUINT & VALKEVICH
ROBERT J. VALKEVICH
MATTHEW D. QUINT
JAMES A. REUBEN
JOEL YODOWITZ
JAMES P. MOHER
LAWRENCE P. SUSSMAN
JEFFREY S. ROSEN
SUSAN C. WEBER

A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SUITE 1330
595 MARKET STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94105

TELEPHONE
(415) 957-9000
FACSIMILE
(415) 957-1727

September 20, 1988

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA  95804

Re: San Franciscans for the USS Missouri - Yes on
    Proposition S Committee
    ID #881499
    RQV File No. 943

Dear Ms. Griffiths:

     On August 4, 1988, a Statement of Organization (Recipient Committee) was filed for the San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S Committee. I am the Treasurer of that organization. The Statement of Organization (Recipient Committee) was amended by a filing on August 25, 1988. On August 10, 1988, the San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S Committee ("Yes on S") received its first contribution in an amount of sixty eight thousand nine hundred twenty seven dollars and twenty eight cents ($68,927.28) from San Franciscans for the Homeporting of the USS Missouri. In an effort to make certain that all of the Yes on S Committee filings are proper, I spoke with Bruce Robeck at the Fair Political Practices Commission on September 1, 1988. I was informed that any money collected from individuals prior to the organization of the Yes on S without any representation or understanding on the part of the donors that monies were being collected in connection with the ballot measure or candidate would not be subject to any reporting requirement of the Fair Political Practices Commission. I was further informed that issues regarding representations to individual donors is a factual good faith question.

     In view of that information, I have taken the opportunity to inquire into and investigate the source of the initial contribution. Those monies were collected at a fundraiser held at the Fairmont Hotel on Wednesday, June 15, 1988. At the time of the fundraiser, none of the organizers nor any of the donors had any idea as to whether or not USS Missouri issues would generate any ballot measure. For your information, I have enclosed copies of all of the relevant documentation including the invitation card sent out to a substantial number of individuals supporting

8000\301075

Diane M. Griffiths
Page 2
September 20, 1988


the homeporting of the USS Missouri. Neither the invitation, the
RSVP card, or any other of the available documentation reflects,
in any way, any reference to a future ballot measure. The
individual organizers of the fundraiser have expressly related
that at the time of the raising of monies, they had no information
or indication that there would be a necessity for ballot
measures.

     Under these circumstances, I have come to the conclusion that
the yes on 5 Committee can accept the sixty eight thousand nine
hundred twenty seven dollars and twenty eight cents ($68,927.28)
donation as received from the San Franciscans for the Homeporting
of the USS Missouri. If you deem otherwise or require any other
information or documentation other than as transmitted with this
correspondence, please let me know. I appreciate your review.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        REUBEN, QUINT & VALKEVICH

                                        By___________________
                                           James A. Reuben

Critics say the U.S.S. Missouri is nuclear powered --
NOT SO! The weapons system it carries is no different
from those carried on ships homeported in Alameda today.
Having nuclear capabilities does not mean nuclear weapons
are present.

Critics say environmental problems would result -- NOT
SO! The extensive environmental impact process has
cleared the project.

Some critics say the Navy discriminates -- AGAIN, NOT
SO! Federal affirmative action and anti-discrimination
standards prevail.

Mayor Art Agnos is saying he will accept the Flotilla
but not the U.S.S. Missouri. In checking with the
Navy on this matter, they point out that this is not
possible because the Flotilla must stay with the
Battleship Missouri.

The U.S. Navy wants the U.S.S. Missouri homeported in
San Francisco. The most recent survey shows that the
majority of the people of San Francisco wants the U.S.S.
Missouri homeported in San Francisco.

Your contribution towards this event will be used to
launch a media campaign which will include newspaper
ads, billboards, radio ads and motorized cable cars, to
generate awareness and support for this cause. A Finance
Committee made up of business and labor will oversee
this program.

Please join us in what should prove to be an informative and exciting event. Your support is vital --
together we will bring the "Mighty Mo" home!

[signatures]

Joseph P. Mazzola          Charles S. Warner
Chairman, Labor            Chairman, Business
Committee                  & Community Committee

[PORT CITY OF THE FUTURE]

THE ECONOMIC BENEFITS OF HOMEPORTING
THE U.S.S. MISSOURI IN SAN FRANCISCO

• After the arrival of the U.S.S. Missouri, the Bay Area will house 17 Naval installations with
a total annual payroll of $1.3 billion per year.

• The U.S.S. Missouri Flotilla will bring 5,500 Navy personnel, with an annual payroll of $110 million
to be spent in San Francisco.

• $58 million annually in ship building and repair contracts.

• $25.6 million spent annually in fuel and supplies.

• An estimated 4,000 new direct and indirect jobs in San Francisco.

• The U.S.S. Missouri attracted 10,000 tourists a day during her last stay the week of July 4th,
1987.

• A $33 million housing package for the construction and refurbishing of homes throughout
the Bay Area, including the construction of 1,500 to 3,000 homes in San Francisco.

.

SAN FRANCISCANS FOR THE
HOMEPORTING OF THE U.S.S. MISSOURI
CORDIALLY INVITES YOU
TO A BENEFIT DINNER
AT THE

or candidate would not be subject to any reporting requirement of

     In view of that information, I have taken the opportunity to

standards prevail.

but not the U.S.S. Missouri. In checking with the
Navy on this matter, they point out that this is not
possible because the Flotilla must stay with the

majority of the people of San Francisco wants the U.S.S.

Your contribution towards this event will be used to
launch a media campaign which will include newspaper
ads; billboards; radio and motorized cable cars, to
generate awareness and support of this cause. A finance
committee made up of business and labor will oversee

Please join us in what should prove to be an informative and exciting evening. Your support is vital --
together we will bring the "Mighty Mo" home!

sections.question:
May a recipient committee receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who made 
payments to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser 
did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their 
payments would be used for political purposes?
sections.conclusion:
A recipient committee may receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who 
contributed to the fundraiser if the contributors to the 
fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of 
their payments would be used for political purposes.
sections.facts:
The San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S 
Committee (the "Proposition S Committee"), for which you serve as 
the treasurer, received a contribution of $68,927.28 from the San 
Franciscans for the Homeporting of the USS Missouri (the 
"Homeporting Committee"). 
The Homeporting Committee held a 
fundraising event to launch a media campaign to generate awareness 
and support for homeporting the USS Missouri in San Francisco 
before Proposition S was proposed as and became a ballot measure. 
l, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
 • (916) 322-5660 


James A. Reuben  
There was no representation to any person who contributed to the 
fundraiser sponsored by the Homeporting Committee that any 
contribution would be used to support or to oppose a ballot 
measure.
sections.analysis:
The issue you raise is the detail of your disclosure 
obligation as treasurer for the Proposition S committee. 
Specifically, you want to know if the Proposition S committee must 
identify each person who contributed $100 or more to a fundraiser 
which was held by the Homeporting Committee prior to the formation 
of your committee and before there was a ballot question. 
A contribution is a payment which has been made by the donor 
for political purposes. 
(Section 82015.) 
A payment is made for 
political purposes if it is for the purpose of influencing or 
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against 
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the 
qualification or passage of any measure. 
(Regulation 18215, copy 
enclosed. ) 
Donations received by an organization, like the Homeporting 
Committee, which is not formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes are "contributions" for purposes of the Act only when, at 
the time of making the payment the donor "knows or has reason to 
know that the payment or funds with which the payment will be 
commingled will be used to make contributions ... expenditures." 
(Regulation 18215(c).) 
In your letter, you indicate that the 
Homeporting Committee did not make any representations to 
potential contributors that they were making payments for the 
political purposes of supporting or opposing a ballot measure or 
any other ballot question. 
Based on this information, we conclude 
that the persons who contributed to the fundraiser did not intend 
to make payments for the purpose of attempting to influence voters 
for or against the qualification or passage of Proposition S. 
Therefore, the Proposition S Committee may receive the 
proceeds of the fundraiser as a contribution from the Homeporting 
Committee without being required to disclose the identity of each 
person who contributed $100 or more to the fundraiser held by the 
Homeporting Committee. 
The Homeporting Committee should be listed 
as the source of the contribution of $68,927.28. 
If the Homeporting Committee is not a recipient committee 
pursuant to Section 82013(a) which has obtained an identification 
number from the Secretary of State, as we understand is the case, 
the Proposition S Committee is required to notify the Homeporting 
Committee that it may be required to file campaign reports. 
(Section 84105.) 
The notice is required to be sent within two 
weeks of receiving the contribution, so if your Committee has not 
already sent a notice to the Homeporting Committee it should do so 
promptly. 


James A. Reuben

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May a recipient committee receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who made 
payments to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser 
did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their 
payments would be used for political purposes?

CONCLUSION: A recipient committee may receive as a contribution the 
proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who 
contributed to the fundraiser if the contributors to the 
fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of 
their payments would be used for political purposes.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission October 21, 1988 James A. Reuben Reuben, Quint and Valkevich 595 Market Street, Suite 1330 San Francisco, CA 94105 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-88-369 Dear Mr. Reuben: You have requested advice regarding your campaign disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ QUESTION May a recipient committee receive as a contribution the proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who made payments to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their payments would be used for political purposes? CONCLUSION A recipient committee may receive as a contribution the proceeds of a fundraiser without identifying the persons who contributed to the fundraiser if the contributors to the fundraiser did not know or have reason to know that all or part of their payments would be used for political purposes. FACTS The San Franciscans for the USS Missouri Yes on Proposition S Committee (the "Proposition S Committee"), for which you serve as the treasurer, received a contribution of $68,927.28 from the San Franciscans for the Homeporting of the USS Missouri (the "Homeporting Committee"). The Homeporting Committee held a fundraising event to launch a media campaign to generate awareness and support for homeporting the USS Missouri in San Francisco before Proposition S was proposed as and became a ballot measure. 1/Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 James A. Reuben Page 2 There was no representation to any person who contributed to the fundraiser sponsored by the Homeporting Committee that any contribution would be used to support or to oppose a ballot measure. ANALYSIS The issue you raise is the detail of your disclosure obligation as treasurer for the Proposition S Committee. Specifically, you want to know if the Proposition S Committee must identify each person who contributed $100 or more to a fundraiser which was held by the Homeporting Committee prior to the formation of your committee and before there was a ballot question. A contribution is a payment which has been made by the donor for political purposes. (Section 82015.) A payment is made for political purposes if it is for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure. (Regulation 18215, copy enclosed.) Donations received by an organization, like the Homeporting Committee, which is not formed or existing primarily for political purposes are "contributions" for purposes of the Act only when, at the time of making the payment the donor "knows or has reason to know that the
